claims.claim_text: The OECD updated its definition of an AI system in 2023 to emphasize machine-based systems that infer from inputs and generate outputs such as predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions.
claims.rationale: The OECD page directly says the definition was revised in 2023 and quotes the updated wording about inferring from inputs to generate predictions, content, recommendations, or decisions.

claims.claim_text: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework was released as version 1.0 in January 2023 and organizes AI risk work around govern, map, measure, and manage functions.
claims.rationale: The NIST Playbook page explicitly states AI RMF 1.0 was released on January 26, 2023 and names the four functions as Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage.

further_reading.title: What is AI? Can you make a clear distinction between AI and non-AI systems?
further_reading.why_relevant: Gives a plain-language explanation of the OECD AI-system definition and how AI differs from explicit hand-written rules.

further_reading.title: NIST AI RMF Playbook
further_reading.why_relevant: Provides a practical control framework for governing, mapping, measuring, and managing AI risk in real operational settings.

further_reading.title: Kubernetes Configuration Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful follow-up for the module's repeated point that generated infrastructure changes still need verification against official platform documentation.

notes: Most of the module is pedagogical framing, hypotheticals, analogies, and design guidance, so it should not generate claim entries. The opening incident, worked example, quiz scenarios, and hands-on exercise are explicitly hypothetical and were intentionally excluded.
